data.title: SentinelOne Looks Like Palantir Before The Breakout (Double Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In every market cycle, there are a handful of high growth companies that the market just doesn’t get - until it does.

In 2021, Palantir (PLTR) was one of those names - dismissed early as a result of the firm's continuous lack of profitability, misunderstood business model, and reliance on government contracts. Following a brief period of irrational exuberance during 2021 and the heyday of Cathie Wood, PLTR's stock went into a multiyear depression, with critics maligning the company's incredibly high stock-based compensation, and dismissing the broader growth opportunity.

Nevertheless, after PLTR began branching out into the commercial market, and following the launch of its incredible AIP functionality, the stock has since gone on an incredible run, increasing from $7 in the early summer of 2023, to more than $150 today - just two years later:

In many ways, PLTR is unique, given its visionary CEO, strong AI product offering, and robust, high quality revenue streams.

That said, we believe that there are a number of other companies on the market that are in a similar position to PLTR in 2023. One of those companies is SentinelOne (NYSE:S).

In case you're unaware, S is a top-flight cybersecurity company that produces an AI-powered endpoint platform called 'Singularity'.

The firm has historically been unprofitable, and stock-based conversation appears high, which is reminiscent of where PLTR was in 2022 and 2023.

Like PLTR, though, we expect that as a number of key technological trends continue to develop, including AI, quantum computing and cybersecurity, we believe that S will be able to begin growing profitably as it begins to unlock operating leverage.

Given that the stock is trading at an incredibly attractive valuation, as S transitions from continuous lossmaking to consistent profitability, we think shares could re-rate, leading to material gains for shareholders.

Today, we'll dive deep into S's business model, outline the company's valuation, and make the case that SentinelOne could be the 'next Palantir' over the coming 12 to 24 months.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

We’ve had our eye on SentinelOne for quite some time.

In the past, we’ve rated the stock a 'Buy', and subsequently a 'Hold' in two separate articles where we called for investors to sell put options on the underlying stock:

  • SentinelOne: Likely A Long-Term Winner
  • SentinelOne: Sell Options For A Double-Digit Yield On This High Flying Cybersecurity Firm

In each of those articles, we made the case that shares weren't likely to increase in price much given the ongoing profitability issues, but that the stock's implied volatility meant that it might be a good opportunity for investors to bring in some option premiums while standing by, ready to snatch up shares at an attractive price.

If you took each of the trades we presented in each of those articles, you’d be holding shares at a combined cost of $17.15. Right now, S is trading at roughly $18, and it's clear there hasn’t been much in the way of sustained material appreciation for shareholders over the last two years.

That said, as the company has continued to deliver quarterly results, we think S is beginning to potentially 'turn the financial corner'.

From a macro level, S is a company defined primarily by two things: strong top line revenue growth (as a result of its high-quality SAAS revenue model), and ongoing bottom-line losses as a result of considerable operating expense growth:

On the top line, in each of the companies last 10 quarters, S has booked top line YoY revenue growth of 23% or higher, highlighting the firm's strong product offering and recurring revenue model:

Not only that, but S's product line remains incredibly sticky due to the deep integration Singularity has within a client's overall cyber and IT systems. In many ways, this mirrors PLTR's deep data integration with customers, making it an indispensable part of any company's operation stack.

Thus, between the fast revenue growth and sticky business model, it’s fair to say that S enjoys similar revenue quality to firms like PLTR.

However, historically, operating expenses have proven difficult to manage, and as you can see from the chart above, OpEx remains high, running at an annual rate actually above revenue.

That said, between growing gross margins and relatively slower opex growth in the past few quarters, S appears to be turning the ship around. This is what has driven S's recent operating results, which seem to have bottomed:

For reference, most IT businesses will consistently grow their operating costs as more revenue comes in, in order to capture market share at the expense of profitability. Then, as companies mature, and growth rates slow down, they limit - or even reduce - operating expenses, locking incredible operating leverage at a companywide level.

This is possible due to the fact that software products have almost zero cost of replication, which leads to very high gross margins and a lot of room for management to control their own profitability/growth lever.

As we see it, S is going through this transformation now, even though growth rates remain high.

To be clear, S has not shown robust profitability yet, with quarterly adjusted earnings of $0.01, $0.00, $0.04, and $0.02 per share over the last year, but it’s a significant improvement versus where S was in 2022 and 2023. This mirrors PLTR's profitability story, which inflected as growth re-accelerated, leading to much stronger bottom-line results.

Similarly, if you dive into S's cash flow statements, you’ll find that over the last 12 months, the business has been producing solid TTM cash from operations, indicating that on a cash basis, investors are no longer funding the company:

Even more encouragingly, free cash flows are even stronger, coming in at $214 million over the last twelve months:

With revenues of only $864 million over the last year, this indicates strong cash conversion, and a free cash flow margin of roughly 25%.

As cash profitability improves, we're expecting SBC to come down, which should push up GAAP net income. Once that happens, we're anticipating a shift in investor perceptions around the company, given S's high gross margins, strong top line growth, and robust FCF.

This is what we saw with PLTR - an initial move that only accelerated as the company's AI product suite saw strong adoption with commercial and federal customers.

For its part, S is already an AI-native product, which provides cybersecurity protection for clients. As awareness improves for this story, we expect the market to rerate the stock.

Valuation

Speaking of the valuation...

While we don’t think the investor community - at large - is generally aware of S's improving profitability story, analysts are forecasting strong top and bottom-line growth over the next few years, with top line increases averaging 20% YoY, and consensus EPS estimates putting 2028 earnings per share at nearly $0.50:

Relative to S's share price around $18, and market cap of less than $6 billion, we think the stock - right now - looks like an absolute steal from a valuation standpoint.

On the top line, nominally, S is trading at only 6x sales, and on the bottom line, S is trading at 27x TTM free cash flow.

Further out in time, projections put S's 2028 top and bottom line multiples at 4x and 36x respectively.

Given S's strong 20%+ projected YoY top line growth, we believe that these multiples are materially mispriced versus the broader market opportunity. It's very rare to find a stock with high quality revenues and strong, 20%+ growth trading at only 6x sales. S's FCF profitability underscores the relative quality here, further exacerbating the issue.

Once SBC clears up, we see huge upside for the stock.

We also see S as being mispriced versus other well-known cybersecurity companies like CrowdStrike (CRWD), and Palo Alto Networks (PANW). S trades on the cheap with forward earnings valuations coming in significantly lower than competitors:

Similarly, when compared on a top line sales basis S is anywhere from 2x to 6x cheaper than peers.

Given that S is projecting forward revenue growth comparable to many of these other companies, and given that S's gross profit margins largely come 'in line' with peers, we think the firm is simply being completely overlooked by the market at present:

Of course, on trailing results, S does have some profitability concerns, which is likely holding down the multiple. However, going forward, as profits begin to emerge more materially, and S's management begins allowing some profit to drop to the bottom line, we think the stock should see its multiple expand to be more in line with other firms with similar profiles.

In some ways, PLTR was a unique story, given that the firm was able to leverage AI products to accelerate revenue growth after period of 'ho hum' results. We don't see S being able to re-accelerate revenues barring some sort of global cyber security attack that spurs demand.

That said, if S can keep executing on its mission and improve bottom line profitability - which appears likely - then we expect shareholders could be in for a PLTR-style move in the stock.

As a result, we’re upgrading our rating for S to 'Strong Buy'.

Risk

Of course, there are risks associated with a position in S.

First off, and perhaps most importantly, S competes in a crowded cybersecurity market against a number of other competitors, including many that are both larger and more well-funded.

This doesn’t concern us too much given that the market for AI cyber security solutions should remain robust and growing in the coming years, but investors shouldn’t expect PLTR-like organic business growth over the long-term, as this is not a blue ocean opportunity.

Additionally, S is a high-growth tech name, and high-growth tech names - in previous market selloffs - have proven to be the 'first to go' at any sign of trouble. That is, investors typically cut bait on their 'expensive' exposure before gutting deeper value positions.

As a result, we believe S will experience heightened volatility through the years until it becomes a more established player in the space.

Summary

Overall, though, we believe that S is an incredibly attractive, asymmetrical investment opportunity given the firm's consistent revenue growth, improving operating leverage, and undemanding valuation.

Of course, there are risks associated with the competitive landscape, and there’s no question that the TAM for the digital twin space - where PLTR operates - are greater vs. cybersecurity.

That said, we believe that as S turns the profitability corner, shares could re-rate, and the stock could expand rapidly higher.

Thus, our 'Strong Buy' rating.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best Contrarian Idea investment competition, which runs through July 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: Sarepta: Despite Restructuring, Downgrading To Sell After Latest Developments
data.description:
Sarepta Therapeutics Overview

The last time I wrote about Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:SRPT), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Sarepta: ELEVIDYS DMD Treatment Setback Brings Downgrade To Hold Rating." With respect to this article, I noted that this company's stock had a Hold rating on the basis of the fact that the company might still be able to recover from the 2 patient deaths of ELEVIDYS as a gene therapy for the treatment of patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD]. The downside here now is that the company was able to partially resolve this issue, in that the FDA has stated that in order to continue to market this therapy, it has to put a black box warning for acute liver injury [ALI] and acute liver failure [ALF].

To that end, my belief is that with this warning label, it could have an impact on revenues to be generated with respect to this in the coming quarters. What didn't happen is the issue of being able to continue to treat non-ambulant DMD patients. The FDA won't allow Sarepta to continue to market ELEVIDYS to this portion of patients until it submits a protocol to the FDA requesting that these non-ambulant patients will need to be given an immunosuppressive regimen with sirolimus. Furthermore, it needs to establish a fair amount of data before it is able to submit such a protocol to the FDA. Specifically, it needs to gather data from a regimen from cohort 8 of the ongoing ENDEAVOR study [Study SRP-9001-103].

This trial is looking at non-ambulant DMD patients and will be necessary for this protocol. The other development that happened was that there was a 3rd patient death, but this dealt with another gene therapy, known as SRP-9004 [patidistrogene bexoparvovec], targeting patients with limb-girdle muscular dystrophy [LGMD]. This gene therapy uses the same AAVrh.74 vector as ELEVIDYS, and thus the reason why the stock declined further today, Friday July 18, 2025.

With reduced revenues on the basis of the black box warning label for ELEVIDYS, I believe this will bring down revenue projections going forward. In terms of a 3rd patient death, it creates uncertainty around the use of the AAVrh.74 vector used in both of the gene therapies it is using to target patients with DMD and LGMD.

With all of these reasons, I must move the rating of SRPT stock from a Hold to a Sell. The restructuring move it did was a wise move, but these other headwinds in place are going to be major issues going forward.

ELEVIDYS Label For Treatment Of DMD Patients Is Not Fully Resolved

One reason on why I have moved this stock to a Sell rating is because the label issue of ELEVIDYS for the treatment of patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy [DMD] was not fully resolved. In essence, the whole problem of the label issue came about with two non-ambulatory DMD patients who died taking it. Both of these deaths prompted the company to stop shipments in this specific patient setting.

If this was the only problem, then the company might have been able to recover, and I believe that this situation would have been rectified. However, this is not all that happened. For starters, because other AAV-delivered gene therapies were already an issue, the FDA required that the company include a black box warning label that patients who take ELEVIDYS might suffer from acute liver injury and acute liver failure. What's the downside, though, with respect to this? The bad news is that this only allows the company to continue to market its drug in the U.S. for only this specific setting. To that end, the label issue relating to the targeting of non-ambulatory DMD patients was not resolved.

The company made a smart move in convening a panel of experts to decide upon what should be done so that the company could possibly continue to market ELEVIDYS as a gene therapy for these non-ambulant DMD patients. The final outcome, which could potentially allow it to continue to market to this subpopulation of patients, is to incorporate an immunosuppressive regimen with sirolimus.

While noble, this has several uncertainties and is why it remains a major risk. To begin with, it must first generate sufficient data from cohort 8 of the ENDEAVOR trial. It must show that such an immunosuppressive regimen given alongside ELEVIDYS doesn't end up causing any major safety concerns. Even if this is ultimately established, this is not the final step. From there, it must submit this protocol data to the FDA, whereby it will ultimately decide if such a move is enough to allow it to market this gene therapy to treat these non-ambulatory DMD patients.

The bottom line here is that there is no assurance that the FDA is going to agree with such a submitted protocol. This is one overhang that creates enormous doubt about revenues to be generated of ELEVIDYS going forward.

Third Patient Death Adds To Further Uncertainty Of Specific Use Of AAV-Gene Therapy Vector

The second reason for my Sell rating on Sarepta Therapeutics is because of a third patient death. Specifically, a 51-year-old man who was enrolled in the phase 1 DISCOVERY study and was given an intravenous [IV] infusion of SRP-9004 [patidistrogene bexoparvovec] for the treatment of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy [LGMD], died. The bad news for Sarepta is that it raises concerns over the entire gene therapy program that it has with respect to its pipeline. How so? Well, the problem is that this other gene therapy, that caused such a death, uses the same AAV-vector of AAVrh.74. Thus, the concern is that this vector could possibly be a cause for concern for ELEVIDYS going forward. Specifically, the patient had died due to acute liver failure, which is the issue that ELEVIDYS suffered from in terms of two patient deaths described above.

The problem with this patient with LGMD who died is that it now casts doubt on the expected Biologics License Application [BLA] filing of SRP-9003 [patidistrogene bexoparvovec] for the treatment of patients with type 2E/R4 disease. Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy [LGMD]. The reason why has to do because of the patient death that occurred. Especially, since this gene therapy uses the very same exact vector of AAV-rh.74 that is used in ELEVIDYS and the other gene therapy candidate SRP-9004. The hope was that the company would be able to submit such a BLA to the FDA to receive approval for SRP-9003. However, this new patient death could possibly give pause or concern to investors in that there is no certainty of whether or not a death will be observed either in the ongoing study or upon a patient being given this gene therapy.

Restructuring Move Brings About Reduced Expenses But Removes Most Of LGMD Pipeline

The third and final reason why I have moved this stock from a Hold to a Sell rating is because of the restructuring it had enacted. While this was a noble move to reduce expenses and headcount, it removes the ability to continue with the other subpopulations of the targeting of LGMD patients with SRP-9004, SRP-9005 and other gene therapies for this entire program. Not only that, but there is no certainty that it will be able to pay off its 2027 convertible note for starters. Plus, the ability to access the up to $600 million in revolving credit facility. The thing is that this restructuring brought about three key events. The first item that was established was the reduction of its workforce by as much as 36%.

This move is expected to allow to bring $120 million in annual cost savings. However, a problem with this is that such cost savings are not expected to happen until the beginning of 2026. Furthermore, the change in its pipeline that it had done is expected to bring in $300 million in annual savings. As I have stated above, the narrow focus of its pipeline does give it focus on fewer therapies, but it removes the ability to advance other gene therapies in its pipeline targeting LGMD patients.

What this means is that, as I will describe below, it is going to have to rely on the development of its RNA interference [RNAi] pipeline. More about this below, but there is risk in that there is no assurance these other ongoing phase 1/2 trial programs are going to end up readout out positive safety/efficacy data upon completion. Getting rid of employees through this intended restructuring cause expenses of severance packages and other one-time cost measures. With that being said, this is only expected to save Sarepta approximately $100 million in savings through the end of 2025.

Early-Stage Pipeline Has Potential But Has Not Established Proof-Of-Concept

The last reason why I believe it is important to have a Sell rating on the stock is because it is too early to state one way or another about whether or not the shift to developing its early-stage RNA interference [RNAi] pipeline will produce substantial results. The three early-stage programs that it wants to continue with the funds it has are as follows:

  • Phase 1/2 study using RNAi candidate SRP-1001 designed to knock down mRNA DUX4 that causes Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, Type 1 [FSHD1] - Preliminary results expected 2nd half of 2025
  • Phase 1/2 study using RNAi candidate SRP-1003 designed to knock down mRNA DMPK in skeletal muscle tissue in patients with Myotonic dystrophy, Type 1 [DM1] - Preliminary phase 1 data expected 2nd half of 2025
  • Phase 1/2 study using RNAi candidate SRP-1004 designed to target toxic ATXN2 protein that causes Spinocerebellar ataxia type 2 [SCA2] - First patient in cohort Q3 of 2025 with future data possibly in 2026.

Other programs in this early-stage development pipeline include the use of RNAi to go after indications like Huntington's Disease [HD] and idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF]. The point here is that there is no assurance that data to be released from these programs in the latter half of this year will be positive or enough to move forward towards phase 2 clinical testing.

SRPT Stock Financials

Sarepta released preliminary guidance on its cash position and financials with the restructuring press release. Its cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments was said to be $850 million as of June 30th of 2025. The truth is that the company is still generating revenue, with the preliminary Q2 of 2025 financials showing that it generated net product revenue of $513 million. This is broken down into net product revenues of ELEVIDYS being $282 million for this quarter and then the RNA-based PMO drugs revenues coming in at $231 million.

Speaking of which, this is where a problem can occur with the patient deaths noted above. While not guaranteed, it is possible that the black box warning label could result in reduced revenue for this gene therapy. Plus, the fact that it won't be able to market to the non-ambulatory DMD patients until it corrects the issue with not only immunosuppressive therapy but getting the FDA to agree to this new protocol measure. While the restructuring is expected to bring in a total of $400 million in annual cost savings, there is no assurance that this and other cost saving measures will be enough to have enough cash flow to pay off its expected 2027 convertible note.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sarepta Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the updated ELEVIDYS label, which includes a black box warning. What it must do for now is only market to ambulant DMD patients with this gene therapy. The risk here is that this new updated label could mean reduce revenue guidance of ELEVIDYS going forward. Plus, this doesn't rule out the possibility that this AAVrh.74 vector could cause additional deaths in the next several months.

The second risk involves the protocol to be submitted to the FDA so that the company could continue to market ELEVIDYS for the treatment of non-ambulatory DMD patients. It believes that it might be able to get a protocol done of adding in immunosuppressive sirolimus with this gene therapy in hopes of reducing unwanted toxicity effects. The risk here is that there is no assurance that data to be generated from cohort 8 of the endeavor study will be good enough to allow it to be able to start selling ELEVIDYS to these non-ambulatory patients again. Even with good safety data on hand, the FDA has to agree that such a protocol will be enough to be able to market the gene therapy for this specific portion of patients.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of the third patient death that occurred in the phase 1 DISCOVERY trial, which used SRP-9004 in the targeting of a 51-year-old patient with Limb-Girdle Muscular Dystrophy [LGMD]. This patient died from acute liver failure and the risk here is that there is the possibility that additional patients could die in this study moving forward. Plus, another risk is that it casts doubt on the expected BLA filing of SRP-9003 for the treatment of patients with type 2E/R4 LGMD in the 2nd half of 2025. Even though the filing of this program would be a positive development, a patient death with SRP-9004 that uses the AAVrh.74 vector will raise concerns about the ability for marketing approval of any gene therapy targeting this group of patients.

The fourth risk to consider would be in terms of the early-stage programs I went over above. The company is shifting specifically to developing the use of RNAi candidates that target indications such as Huntington's Disease [HD], idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis [IPF], Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy, Type 1 [FSHD1], Myotonic dystrophy, Type 1 [DM1] and Spinocerebellar ataxia type 2 [SCA2]. There is the expected release of results for several of these indications in the 2nd half of 2025. The risk here is that there is no assurance that data, to be released from early-stage studies targeting these indications, are going to be positive. Nor that they will result in the company's ability to move these programs towards the next stage of clinical development.

The fifth and final risk to consider would be the restructuring plan that it has put into motion to conserve cash. First of all, this move entails removing the development of the other gene therapies that were being developed to target other LGMD patient types. In addition, the hope is that the $400 million annual cost savings implemented and other measures are going to be enough to allow the company to meet its 2027 debt obligations. The risk here is that there is no assurance that the measures it has taken to reduce expenses will be enough to meet such expected obligations.

Conclusion

I have moved SRPT from a Hold rating to a Sell rating because of several bad key developments that have transpired since I last wrote about it. I thought that it would be able to resolve the marketing issue of ELEVIDYS in terms of the two patient deaths that occurred. However, the label now has to include that this gene therapy may end up causing acute liver failure and acute liver injury. I believe that this action is going to end up resulting in reduce revenues because of it. Plus, what also did not help was the recent release of a third patient death that dealt with the phase 1 DISCOVERY trial focused on treating patients with LGMD.

This raises doubts and concerns of continuing to market a gene therapy that uses the AAVrh.74 vector going forward. If Sarepta can correct the issue of the FDA agreeing to the immunosuppressive protocol of ELEVIDYS and be able to market non-ambulatory DMD patients, plus it doesn't see any additional patient deaths within a 1-year period, then I might be inclined to move SRPT stock to a Hold rating. However, until such time, I believe that this stock has too much uncertainty going for it and until this is cleared up, then I believe it should be a Sell rating.

data.title: Milestone Pharmaceuticals Remains A Buying Opportunity Despite Recent Dilution
data.description:
Overview

Our last article on Milestone Pharmaceuticals Inc. (NASDAQ:MIST), which saw tremendous interest from the community, discussed the solid clinical data set and market potential of Milestone's intranasally administered calcium channel blocker, etripamil. Milestone experienced a major setback, receiving a Complete Response Letter (CRL) to their application. Additionally, Milestone announced on 11 June a $52.5 million public offering comprised of 31.5 million packaged shares, “Series A” warrants, and “Series B” warrants. Immediately following the CRL, shares fell 60%+ on 28 March and continued to decline until 8 April, bottoming at a price per share of $0.65. Milestone had initially remained very quiet on their plan of resubmission and did not release many further details regarding the situation, fueling investors’ doubts. We took the opportunity to reinforce our position, buying into the route to decrease our cost average as our investment thesis had not been significantly impacted by the CRL, as no safety or efficacy issues were highlighted. Since then, the stock price has steadily recovered to $2.50 but was impacted by the recent dilution. In this article, we provide our view on the nature of the Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Control (CMC) problems behind the CRL and consider the effect of the equity dilution announced. We also update our detailed financial model to reflect both of these updates.

At this time, we remain optimistic about the future potential of etripamil in the PSVT and adjacent indication markets. The thesis remains intact. However, while the CRL provided a great buying opportunity, the recent dilution has negatively impacted the potential upside, slashing the upside roughly in half. We will also talk about how this could be viewed as a potential long-term strategic de-risking strategy on behalf of management.

Updates to the financial model

While a detailed discussion of the events that have followed our prior article will follow, we will first update our financial modeling on Milestone, considering the following changes:

  1. The new equity offering and potential exercise prices of the warrants sold along with the shares
  2. The new timeline of approval, with commercialization happening in 2026.
  3. A higher discount rate.

A detailed explanation of the logic and assumptions used to construct the original model is detailed both in our previous Seeking Alpha publication and our deep dive. Rather than repeating these explanations in this article, we refer readers wishing to better understand the way in which we have modeled Milestone to those resources.

We have raised our Milestone discount rate to 20%. This decision aims to incorporate the unmodeled uncertainty of launch performance as well. We also aim to reflect lower confidence in the management team; failure to appropriately prepare nitrosamine testing casts a bad light upon the regulatory team (especially in combination with the 2023 refusal to file letter), and recent unnecessary dilution are contribution factors. The effects of this change will be further discussed on a case-by-case basis in each of the scenarios we consider.

Equity offering terms

Milestone is raising $52 million from the sale of:

  1. 31.5 million shares accompanied by (i) 31.5 million “Series A” warrants and (ii) 31.5 million “Series B” warrants, priced at $1.50 altogether.
  2. 3.5 million pre-funded warrants, accompanied by (i) 31.5 million “Series A” warrants and (ii) 31.5 million “Series B” warrants.

As is outlined in the prospectus, Series A warrants expire one year following issuance, while Series B warrants expire five years following issuance. Series A warrants have an exercise price of $1.50, while the Series B warrants have an exercise price of $1.875. Milestone has the added option to force exercise of the Series B warrants if the 10-day volatility weighted average price (VWAP) of the stock exceeds $3.50.

Estimating cash burn in 2025

In Q1, Milestone reported increased commercial expenses as they were preparing for the launch of etripamil following the PDUFA at the end of March. We model that these expenses can be partially offset to roughly half of their Q1 values during Q2 and Q3, before returning to $10 million in Q4 2025 for the repeated commercialization preparations. We keep the same cash burn in the remainder of the year as was seen in Q1 for R&D and G&A expenses. The result is a roughly forecast net loss of $70 million for FY 2025, which we approximate for cash burn. This reduces the cash balance (before considering additional fundraising) from $55.9 million reported in Q1 to around $5 million, uncomfortably low. While this calls for a small amount of additional funds to be raised leading up to approval, the size of the equity offering announced today is significantly bigger, and it was done at very bad terms. It would have been, in our view, more appropriate to conduct a small equity offering (or to utilize the existing ATM program) to raise this additional cash, with a subsequent offering happening at a higher valuation post-approval if more funds are required for the commercialization efforts.

Updating the base case

As a reminder, our base case uses our more conservative pricing estimate and Milestone’s guided prevalence/incidence numbers. Our base case previously used a price point of $70/episode for CARDAMYST, which was calculated by implying a price point based on the conditions of the royalty financing deal. We discuss this methodology in detail in our previous article and deep dive. Increasing the discount rate to 20% affects this calculation and increases the implied price to $140. Fig. 2 summarizes our updated etripamil price estimate.

We have shifted the launch year to 2026 and added the previously calculated cash burn estimations for 2025. We have also reflected the increased Total Shares Outstanding (TSO), resulting in the dilution: 53.4 million shares previously outstanding (Q1 2025) + 31.5 million new shares issued + 12.9 pre-funded warrants previously outstanding + 3.5 million new pre-funded warrants issued. In our calculation of diluted total shares outstanding (diluted TSO), we have included the 35 million Series A and 35 million Series B warrants. The proceeds from the sale of shares at $1.50 have been added to the EOY 2025 cash balance, which also includes the estimated cash burn from this year. The warrants will be assumed to exercise following approval at their respective exercise prices.

In Fig. 3, we can see that the cash balance in 2026 and thereafter significantly exceeds necessary working capital levels. It is only necessary to assemble such a large position to fund a phase 3 trial in the atrial fibrillation indication and inefficient otherwise. It would also have been significantly more efficient to split this fundraising into several smaller rounds, a $10-20 million raise to provide liquidity prior to approval, and further rounds at a higher valuation post-approval. However, by conducting a larger round now, Milestone guarantees that funds will be available regardless of approval—a significant de-risking strategy.

We value NPV using the same methodology as our previous valuation, but with the updated share count (with all the warrants being exercised upon approval) and a higher discount rate. The differences are shown in Fig. 4. We can see a decrease in the fair value in the case of approval (potential upside) from $5.60 to $2.33. The market-implied probability of approval, which we discuss in detail in our previous article, has increased from 30.40% to 58.45% as the upside and premium to cash basis have decreased. Investors who believe that the likelihood of approval (LOA) is below the updated 57.9% should consider selling their positions. We currently estimate a likelihood of approval around 80%, and we will adjust for this later in the article.

Mid-range scenario

We perform the same updates to our mid-range case, wherein we use the $500/episode price point Milestone management has indicated they will price etripamil at, but dampened prevalence/incidence estimates are more in line with non-affiliated academic research into PSVT prevalence/incidence. The results can be seen in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6.

The mid-range case indicates that if Milestone can commercialize at their intended price point, significant upside still remains. Again, we do roughly estimate that the implied probability of approval is still much greater than 50.76%, at around 80%.

Blue Sky scenario

Finally, we perform the same updates to our “blue-sky” case, wherein we use the $500/episode price point Milestone management has indicated they will price etripamil and management’s incidence/prevalence estimates. The results can be seen in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8 and indicate that plenty of upside remains.

Risk-adjusted fair value

The above valuations determine the fair price that Milestone should be assigned upon approval. However, the fair value today should reflect a likelihood of approval of around 80%, with only potential further CMC/compliance problems (and no safety/efficacy) problems contributing to the 20% probability discount. These valuations are given for our three cases in Fig. 9:

As we can see, despite the significant dilution, there remains sufficient upside to remain invested in the long-term future of the company.

With the modeling out of the way, let’s get down to the details on what’s changed about the fundamentals of the company.

More details on the events following our previous article

Milestone received a CRL from the FDA on 28 March, which gave no concerns regarding the safety or efficacy of etripamil, but highlighted the following CMC issues to be addressed:

Company to submit additional information on nitrosamine impurities based on new draft guidance issued after the NDA submission; and

An inspection is required at a facility that performs release testing for etripamil, to ensure it is in compliance with Current Good Manufacturing Practices. The facility changed ownership during the review of the NDA.

Milestone has specifically emphasised that the lack of concerns over safety or efficacy is a little bit of positive news for the company against the disappointing background of the setback.

After completing “ongoing assays to address the new impurity testing requirement” in April, Milestone requested a Type A meeting with the FDA on 14 May. The FDA, obligated to respond within 30 days, had this meeting with Milestone on 15 June. Milestone resubmitted their application the following day, on 16 June. On 11 July, Milestone announced their new PDUFA date of 13 December. On the same day, they announced that they had extended the date of expiry of the royalty agreement with RTW Investments through to 31 December 2025, to accommodate the new PDUFA date. We will discuss this later in the article.

Let’s talk about what was in the CRL.

CMC problems

Milestone is not alone in dealing with an increased prevalence of CMC issues. For the most part, these are generally resolvable, acting as more of a setback than a death warrant. As a primer for our discussion of these details, consider some words of wisdom on recent CMC issues from Leonard ((Len)) Schleifer, co-founder, President, CEO of Regeneron Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (REGN), and the longest-serving pharmaceutical CEO, with a tenure of 37 years:

The kinds of issues that have come up are reflecting, in my view, an increased scrutiny by the FDA post-COVID on contract manufacturers performing various functions. All of our CRLs -- I should say not all, but for the vast majority of our CRLs, they relate to these issues at third-party suppliers, which the FDA recognized were woefully behind the times during COVID. There wasn't enough of them. They were flunking inspections. And so I think the FDA is trying to step up the game, if you will, of these contract manufacturers. And since we're so active, we see more. I don't think it's a -- I'll acknowledge for sure that we're unhappy about this. And if there's a blame, I'm happy to take it personally, but it's certainly not the reflection on our regulatory group and our manufacturing people who are working really hard to get this right. - Leonard Schleifer, Q1 2025 earnings call

There are two issues at hand: the inspection and the impurities. In terms of the facility reinspection, there is no insight to offer. We will trust that the third-party manufacturers, big and competent players, will be able to coordinate with the FDA to arrange this. Given that to be awarded a resubmission, you have to provide a complete response to the issues highlighted in the CRL, we assume this has already taken place.

Some investors had voiced dissatisfaction with the relative lack of information immediately following the CRL. Though this has slowly improved, there is still not much light on many topics. We believe this is primarily because Milestone would not be party to much information either. Let’s head back to the words of Len to shed some more light on why.

“When the FDA is reviewing your submission for an approval of a new device, we don't necessarily make all the components. And in this case, we don't make all the components. You might be buying a stopper from somebody, some glass from somebody else, and needle from somebody else and so forth. And we have the design and then we have an assembly.

When the FDA has a question about one of the components, that's what's referred to as a drug master file. They go to the holder of that drug master file. Let's say it's somebody who makes one of the components. The FDA has a question, "Well, how do you guys do this?" And then the holder of the master file responds to the FDA.

By rule, we are not party to that up and back between the FDA and the third-party component supplier. The reason we're not party is that most of the time, these questions relate to general practices where -- in which the supplier is not only supplying Regeneron, they might be supplying 20 other pharmaceutical companies, which is, in fact, the case in some of these DMFs we're dealing with here. So that's the general tone of things.”

Nitrosamine impurities

Nitrosamines are chemical compounds bearing a common functional group: >N–N=O. They can form either as the result of Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) manufacturing processes, be introduced as contaminants in precursor materials, or be related to the drug product (the delivery vehicle of the drug—the nasal spray). There are two main types of nitrosamines, which the current version of guidance differentiates between small-molecule nitrosamines and nitrosamine drug substance-related impurities (NDSRIs), which share structural similarities to the API.

The main update to these guidelines came in September 2024, after the submission of Milestone’s NDA in May 2024. The differing risk assessment and testing requirements between these two guidelines are, in our opinion, the likely cause of this recent CRL.

This class of compounds is broad and impurities could have many sources. Both API and drug product manufacturing could be a source. Either way, the multiple classes of nitrosamine impurities and multiple potential sources of origin do make it difficult for investors to speculate as to the potential source/type of impurities.

Pessimistically, NDSRIs may inherently form in the manufacturing process of etripamil and a complete manufacturing process overhaul could be necessary. We think it is this initial fear that sent the stock so low. We don’t think this is likely.

Optimistically, the delay could have been caused by a lack of testing documentation required by the new guidance on behalf of a third-party manufacturer. In this case, a package of additional data would be prepared and submitted to comply with the new regulations. We believe the wording of the CRL, “submit additional information on nitrosamine impurities,” indicates that it is likely a testing/compliance issue. We don’t think this was initially fully picked up by the media – “Milestone will have to address a problem with nitrosamine impurities,” for instance. This would be incorrect if we are talking about additional confirmatory testing.

Indeed, there was talk of this nitrosamine testing (mentioned previously) which was ongoing during April and due to finish in May. We speculate that Milestone may have received this data in early to mid-May, submitting the request for the Type A meeting shortly thereafter on 14 May, with the meeting happening 30 days later on 15 June. On 16 June, they announced that they had resubmitted their application. In our opinion, this fast turnaround indicates that the issue was likely a testing compliance problem, as there would not have been enough time to fix impurity problems by finding new suppliers or changing the manufacturing process in the case that elevated levels of impurities were present.

Precedent with neffy, ARS Pharmaceuticals

ARS Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (SPRY) is a commercial-stage biotechnology company that recently had its lead candidate, neffy, approved for the treatment of allergic reactions, including anaphylaxis. Neffy is an intranasally administered epinephrine spray and shares many similarities with Milestone. It is a single-product company that very quickly developed a novel product based on a very well-known and trusted compound and clinical approach. By simply modifying the method of administration, SPRY has created an enterprise value of close to $1.5 billion and has entered into a market infamous for supply constraints and high pricing, offering patients an alternative and cheaper treatment option.

ARS Pharmaceuticals also suffered a CRL in September 2023. An additional study was requested, and “requested additional information on nitrosamine impurities to be tested for”. The company managed to resubmit its application within 7 months of the CRL and was granted approval 4 months later. All in all, it took 11 months for neffy to go from CRL to approval. In contrast, Milestone (dealing with lighter issues) took just two and a half months to complete the task of resubmission.

Conclusion

While Milestone has faced significant setbacks from a CRL and a subsequent, poorly timed and poorly priced dilutive equity offering, the fundamental investment thesis for etripamil remains positive. Our updated financial model, accounting for the dilution and new timelines, shows a reduced risk-adjusted fair value of $1.88 but still promises alpha from the current stock price. In the case of approval, we expect upside of around $1/share or more at the current share price, $1.38.

While the reduction in PSVT exposure has hurt our model, we must consider management’s motivations in doing so. Milestone no longer faces imminent shutdown if (for whatever reason) they fail to secure approval in December and would be able to pivot to another resubmission or finance the completion of the atrial fibrillation program, regardless.

data.title: Understanding VT: Vanguard's All-In-One Global Stock ETF
data.description:
What is VT?

Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:VT) is an exchange‑traded fund that seeks to track the FTSE Global All Cap Index, thus mirroring nearly the entire global stock market. That coverage spans developed and emerging markets; it also includes large, mid, and small‑cap firms. Launched in June 2008, VT still provides broad equity exposure through a single, passively managed vehicle; moreover, stock selection involves minimal human intervention. At the same time, and true to Vanguard’s core philosophy, the fund carries an annual expense ratio of just 0.06%. Consequently, it remains one of the most cost‑effective ways to access global equities.

What Does VT Offer Investors?

VT delivers comprehensive global diversification by spreading investments across thousands of companies in numerous countries. Because the fund buys more of the biggest companies (measured by their total stock‑market value), a lot of its money ends up in U.S. stocks. Still, it doesn’t ignore the rest of the world; it puts a meaningful chunk into Europe, Asia, and fast‑growing developing countries. Spreading money around like this cuts risk because you’re not betting on just one region. That said, most of those holdings are in large, well‑established companies, not in smaller, more speculative firms.

When it comes to performance, VT has posted steady growth over time. Over the past year, it generated strong returns that tracked global equity markets; moreover, since inception, it has produced mid‑single‑digit average annualized growth, a figure that mirrors the long‑term upward trend of global markets. Volatility lines up with well‑diversified large‑cap equity benchmarks. There are occasional moderate drawdowns; nevertheless, the ride is generally smoother than what you find in single‑country funds.

VT keeps costs and taxes low. Because it’s an ETF, you can buy or sell it any time the market is open, and it usually doesn’t hand you big taxable payouts like many mutual funds do. Vanguard is huge, so the gap between its buying and selling prices is tiny, and there’s plenty of trading activity to get in or out easily. Lately, rising stock markets worldwide, especially in Europe and Japan, have boosted the fund, though U.S. shares still make up the biggest slice. Finally, Vanguard’s broader cost‑cutting move in early 2025 reduced fees across many funds; however, VT’s already ultra‑low fee stayed virtually unchanged.

Who Might Consider the Vanguard Total World Stock Index Fund?

Think of VT as an all‑in‑one stock fund. Instead of making you pick separate funds for the U.S., Europe, Asia, and so on, it bundles almost the entire world’s stock markets into a single package. That makes life easier if you just want broad coverage without doing extra homework. On top of that, it’s cheap to own and easy to buy or sell, so it works well both for people who plan to hold it for many years and for those who like to tweak their portfolios from time to time.

For investors who already hold concentrated positions, most commonly in U.S. equities, VT can, importantly, act as a broad complement. It spreads some of your investment into Europe, Asia, and other parts of the world where prices and market forces aren’t the same as in the U.S. This approach keeps you from stacking all your chips on a single bet. Conversely, if you’d rather drill down on something more precise, like fast‑growing small‑caps, dividend‑rich stocks, or one particular country, VT can seem too broad since it skims those areas instead of zeroing in on them.

What’s Inside VT? A Closer Look at Sector Allocation and its Top Holdings

To better understand how VT’s broad mandate translates into actual exposure, we’ll now take a closer look at its top five holdings and examine what each contributes to the fund’s overall structure.

1. Microsoft Corporation

Microsoft (MSFT) makes up about 3.7% of the VT fund, making it the fund’s single biggest stock holding. The company first became famous for Windows and Office, but under its current boss, Satya Nadella, it has reinvented itself. Today, it’s a major force in cloud computing (Azure), business software and social networking (Office 365 and LinkedIn), and video games (Xbox and its purchase of Activision Blizzard). Moreover, a decisive push into artificial intelligence, now fused through many of its platforms, illustrates Microsoft’s adaptability along with strong financial discipline, healthy profit margins, and steady shareholder returns via dividends and buybacks, keeping the stock at the core of many long‑term portfolios.

2. NVIDIA Corporation

Meanwhile, NVIDIA (NVDA) Corporation, at 3.45% of the fund, ranks just behind Microsoft in VT’s lineup, proving that a focused tech niche can become the backbone of today’s digital economy. Founded in 1993, the company started out making high‑end graphics chips for gamers, yet those same GPUs now drive AI projects, scientific modeling, and other serious high‑performance computing jobs. Because AI is booming, NVIDIA’s hardware now runs everything from huge data centers to self‑driving cars and big AI‑training systems. The market noticed: by mid-2025, the company’s total stock value briefly topped $4 trillion, an industry first, showing how central it has become. Under longtime CEO Jensen Huang, NVIDIA keeps moving fast and executing well, though its sky‑high price tag means the stock can swing up and down. Even so, its tech is so critical to the next wave of computing that VT gives it an important place in the fund.

3. Apple Inc.

Apple Inc. (AAPL) makes up about 3.23% of the fund. It contributes a different kind of tech leadership, one built on seamless ecosystem integration and strong consumer loyalty. Since launching in 1976, Apple has gone from personal computer pioneer to shaping how billions talk, work, and stream through the iPhone, iPad, Mac, and Apple Watch. Each device ties into services like Apple Music, iCloud, and the App Store, thus creating a cycle that keeps users and their subscription dollars coming back. Thanks to this setup, Apple’s sales keep growing. It generates plenty of cash, and it spends that money wisely. The catch is that a big chunk of its revenue still depends on iPhone sales. And its newer bets on augmented reality and artificial intelligence could be risky. Still, under CEO Tim Cook, Apple is an iconic brand that merges new ideas with solid day‑to‑day management, so many investors see it as a reliable, well‑known piece of VT’s portfolio.

4. Amazon.com, Inc.

Amazon (AMZN) makes up a little over 2% of the VT fund. It started as an online bookstore back in 1994, but now it does just about everything in tech and retail around the world. The online shopping site still brings in the most sales dollars, yet most of the company’s profits actually come from Amazon Web Services (AWS), its cloud computing business. AWS is the top provider of rented, on‑demand computing power for everyone from tiny startups to huge global companies. At the same time, Amazon keeps rolling out new ideas in package delivery, artificial intelligence, video streaming, and smart‑home gadgets, blending in‑store and online shopping. CEO Andy Jassy leads a company noted for reinvestment even at the expense of short‑term profits. Regulatory scrutiny, fierce competition, and economic sensitivity persist, but Amazon’s scale, infrastructure, and tech edge make it a key piece of VT’s global mix.

5. Meta Platforms, Inc.

Finally, Meta Platforms (META) makes up about 1.6% of the VT fund. You probably know it as the company behind Facebook, Instagram, WhatsApp, Messenger, and Threads, apps that connect billions of people and make most of their money by selling ads. Meta is also pouring a lot of cash into new tech like artificial intelligence and the “metaverse” through its Reality Labs division. These are big, expensive strategies that could pay off later. CEO Mark Zuckerberg keeps pouring resources into data infrastructure and AI despite public scrutiny, tighter rules, and tough competition. Even so, Meta’s flagship apps are woven into everyday life, so the company stays relevant, and that’s why VT holds a solid position in it.

VT Performance Overview

When people talk about its “momentum,” they’re really asking, “Has this ETF been gaining value faster than other funds and for how long?”

The answer is yes.

Over the last year, VT grew about 14.4% in total value. That’s roughly 74% better than the middle‑of‑the‑pack ETF.

Stretch the window to three years, and your money would have piled up about 61.8%; give it five years, and you’re looking at almost an 84% increase. In other words, the fund has been beating the average for quite a while.

How does it compare with the famous S&P 500 index of big U.S. companies?

Surprisingly well.

Over one, three, and five years, VT has kept pace. True, if you look way back over ten years, the S&P 500 still wins. But that long‑term gap doesn’t change the main point: owning a globally diversified fund like VT hasn’t forced investors to sacrifice growth. It’s still been a solid, long‑run choice.

The really impressive thing about VT isn’t just that it beat other funds once; it keeps doing so. Over the past one to three years, its price gains blew past the typical ETF by such a wide margin that Seeking Alpha gave it an A‑ for its most recent six‑month stretch, during which it outperformed the average fund by about 249%.

Why?

As previously noted, a lot of VT’s money is in big, fast‑growing companies around the world, especially in tech, and that blend has benefited from both a weaker U.S. dollar and the ongoing strength of large technology firms. At the same time, VT spreads its investments widely: only about 19% of its assets are tied up in its ten biggest holdings. Because the fund isn’t overloaded with just a few stocks, a bad earnings report from one company won’t torpedo the whole portfolio. That broad spread also keeps VT’s day‑to‑day price swings below the ETF average, so investors get a smoother ride whenever markets get volatile.

On the other hand, total return is not the only goal for every saver. Many savers, especially retirees, also want steady cash coming in. That cash usually arrives as dividends or interest. But when prices keep rising via inflation, a small dividend doesn’t go very far.

That’s where VT falls a bit short.

VT’s distribution yield is just 1.76%. Its three‑year compound dividend growth rate of 3.49% pales beside a peer median north of 8%, and its ten‑year record is equally subdued.

It's worth noting that VT has sent a dividend check every year for sixteen straight years, so you can count on getting something. Still, if you’re looking for bigger or faster‑growing income, this fund probably won’t impress you. You might decide to pick a different fund that pays more, even if that other fund charges higher fees or owns fewer kinds of stocks.

Fees can quietly eat away at your investment gains, but VT keeps them tiny. The fund charges just 0.06% a year, compared with about 0.50% for the average ETF, so it gets top marks for being cheap. You can also buy or sell it without much extra cost, because the difference between the buying price and selling price is only about 0.01%.

Even small fees compound over time. Trim roughly half a percentage point from your annual costs, and, a couple of decades down the line, the savings can be substantial. VT makes that easier: it turns over only about 3% of its portfolio each year, versus roughly 30% at a typical fund, so taxes and trading costs stay low. On top of that, it hugs its benchmark, deviating by less than 1% (tracking error for both one and five years), so you get the market’s return without unwanted surprises.

Should You Invest in VT?

Overall, Vanguard’s Total World Stock ETF offers everyday investors a simple, low‑cost way to own a slice of almost every major stock market, pairing respectable long‑term growth with below‑average volatility and razor‑thin fees that let compounding do the heavy lifting. Because it spreads your money across thousands of companies and never puts too much into any single one, it has grown nicely over the years without swinging wildly up and down, and it’s even kept pace with the famous S&P 500 during shorter stretches. The catch is that it doesn’t pay out much in dividends, so it won’t please anyone who needs steady income from their investments. But if you’re the type who just wants to buy a fund, leave it alone, and let low fees and long‑term growth do the work, VT can be a solid core holding, giving you room to add more specialized funds on the side if you want.

This article answers these three main questions about VT:

  1. Is VT a good ETF for diversifying a portfolio?
  2. Does VT appeal to income investors?
  3. Is VT more or less volatile than the S&P 500?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.
